01-TRACE-A-REQUEST-END-TO-END.title: 01-TRACE-A-REQUEST-END-TO-END
01-TRACE-A-REQUEST-END-TO-END.content:
Trace A Request End To End
The fastest way to learn SaaS infrastructure is to follow one request until it becomes boring. Every intimidating term is just a device on the path: naming, routing, encrypting, authenticating, authorizing, computing, storing, caching, observing, retrying, and rolling back.
Cross-links: networking, auth, backend design, state, observability.
The Request Path
sequenceDiagram
  participant U as User
  participant B as Browser
  participant DNS as DNS Resolver
  participant CDN as CDN / Edge
  participant LB as Load Balancer
  participant APP as App Server
  participant AUTH as Auth / Session Store
  participant DB as DB / Cache / Queue
  participant OBS as Logs / Metrics / Traces

  U->>B: click / submit / fetch()
  B->>DNS: resolve app.example.com
  DNS-->>B: IP / CNAME chain
  B->>CDN: TCP or QUIC + TLS handshake
  CDN->>LB: HTTP request
  LB->>APP: route to healthy backend
  APP->>AUTH: validate cookie/token/session
  AUTH-->>APP: principal + permissions
  APP->>DB: read/write state
  DB-->>APP: result
  APP->>OBS: trace span + logs + metrics
  APP-->>B: HTTP response + headers
  B-->>U: render updated UI

What Each Hop Decides




























































HopThe question it answersFailure signatureBrowser/appWhat origin, method, headers, cookies, body?CORS error, missing cookie, malformed JSONDNSWhich IP or edge should receive this name?NXDOMAIN, stale address, slow first requestTCP/QUICCan bytes flow reliably enough?timeout, reset, packet loss, head-of-line blockingTLSWho am I talking to, and is traffic encrypted?cert error, handshake failure, old protocolCDN/edgeCan this be cached or blocked at the edge?stale content, WAF false positiveLoad balancerWhich healthy backend gets this request?502/503, uneven backend loadApp serverWhat business action should happen?500, bad validation, race conditionAuth/sessionWho is this, and what can they do?401, 403, CSRF, confused sessionData/cache/queueWhere is durable or shared state?stale read, deadlock, duplicate jobObservabilityCan we explain this later?no trace ID, no user-impact metric
The Three Planes
Most cloud diagrams become easier when separated:
data plane:     user requests and responses
control plane:  deploys, config, routing rules, autoscaling
identity plane: users, services, roles, tokens, secrets
Common confusion comes from mixing them. For example, a Kubernetes Ingress is control plane config that changes data plane routing. OAuth login uses the identity plane to create credentials that the data plane later presents.
Minimal Request Debugging Script
When someone says “the app is down,” ask:
dig app.example.com
curl -v app.example.com/healthz
curl -v -H "Host: app.example.com" https://203.0.113.10/healthz
Then split the failure:

DNS fails: name resolution problem.
TLS fails: certificate, SNI, protocol, trust chain.
Edge returns 403: WAF, auth, geo/IP rule, missing header.
LB returns 502: backend connection problem.
App returns 500: application or dependency problem.
App returns 200 but UI broken: frontend asset/API/session/CORS problem.

First-Principle Devices
A Request Is A Contract
The contract is:
method + URL + headers + body + credentials + timeout + retry policy
If two systems disagree on any part, the bug may look “networky” while being a contract bug.
Latency Is A Budget
For a 300 ms page interaction:
DNS 20 ms
TLS 50 ms
edge/LB 10 ms
app 80 ms
database/cache 50 ms
render/client JS 90 ms
Every retry or extra round trip spends the same budget. Authentication redirects, CORS preflights, and cold starts matter because they add round trips.
State Is Gravity
Stateless app servers are easy to scale because any request can go anywhere. The moment you add sessions, file uploads, in-memory locks, or local caches, placement and failover matter.
Exercise
Pick one real endpoint you use and fill this in:
URL:
Method:
Request headers that matter:
Cookies/tokens sent:
Authentication decision:
Authorization decision:
Database/cache calls:
External API calls:
Response status:
Response headers that matter:
Trace/log correlation key:
Expected p95 latency:
Retry behavior:
If you can do this for five endpoints, SaaS infrastructure starts becoming muscle memory.
Sources

MDN HTTP
Kubernetes Service
Kubernetes Ingress
OpenTelemetry docs

02-NETWORKING-HTTP-DNS-TLS.title: 02-NETWORKING-HTTP-DNS-TLS
02-NETWORKING-HTTP-DNS-TLS.content:
Networking, HTTP, DNS, And TLS
For SaaS work, you do not need to become a router engineer. You need to know which layers explain which symptoms, and which knobs are dangerous. The useful stack is: DNS names things, IP routes packets, TCP/QUIC moves bytes, TLS authenticates/encrypts, HTTP carries application semantics.
Cross-links: request trace, auth, security, operations.
The Layers That Matter
flowchart TD
  HTTP["HTTP: methods, headers, status, caching, cookies"]
  TLS["TLS: server identity, encryption, certs, ALPN"]
  TCP["TCP or QUIC: connection, loss, congestion, streams"]
  IP["IP: routing between networks"]
  DNS["DNS: name -> address / edge"]
  HTTP --> TLS --> TCP --> IP
  DNS --> HTTP

Mental shortcut:

DNS bug: “I cannot find where to go.”
TCP/QUIC bug: “I cannot establish or maintain a path.”
TLS bug: “I do not trust who answered, or we cannot agree how to encrypt.”
HTTP bug: “The application contract was rejected or failed.”

DNS
DNS resolves a name to records:

A / AAAA: IPv4/IPv6 address.
CNAME: alias to another name.
MX: email routing.
TXT: verification, SPF/DKIM/DMARC, miscellaneous metadata.

Practical gotchas:

TTL controls how long resolvers may cache. Low TTL helps migration but increases query volume.
DNS changes are not instant because caches exist at many layers.
A CNAME chain can hide where traffic really enters: CDN, load balancer, or vendor.
Split-horizon DNS can return different answers inside a VPC than from the public internet.

Commands:
dig app.example.com
dig +trace app.example.com
dig CNAME app.example.com
TCP, QUIC, And Timeouts
TCP gives reliable ordered bytes. QUIC gives reliable encrypted streams over UDP and avoids some TCP head-of-line limitations.
For app engineers, the important knobs are:

connection timeout: how long to wait to connect.
request timeout: how long the whole operation may take.
idle timeout: how long a connection can sit unused.
keepalive: whether connections are reused.
max connections: resource cap.
retry policy: whether to try again after failure.

Failure mode: a client has a 60 second timeout, a load balancer has a 30 second idle timeout, and the app holds the request for 45 seconds. The client sees a mysterious reset or 502, not the app’s intended response.
TLS
TLS provides:

server authentication via certificates.
encryption in transit.
integrity: traffic cannot be modified without detection.
optional client authentication via mTLS.

TLS is usually terminated at:

CDN/edge.
load balancer.
ingress gateway.
service mesh proxy.
the app itself.

Each termination point is a trust boundary. After TLS terminates, traffic may be re-encrypted or travel inside a private network. Do not casually say “TLS means secure” without knowing where it terminates.
Typical production chain:
browser --TLS--> CDN --TLS--> load balancer --TLS or private network--> app
Headers to understand:
Strict-Transport-Security: max-age=31536000; includeSubDomains
X-Forwarded-Proto: https
X-Forwarded-For: client, proxy1, proxy2
Forwarded: for=...;proto=https;host=...
Gotcha: never trust forwarded headers from arbitrary clients. Trust them only from your known proxy/load balancer boundary.
HTTP Semantics
Methods:

GET: safe read. Should not change state.
POST: create/action, not necessarily idempotent.
PUT: replace resource, usually idempotent.
PATCH: partial update.
DELETE: delete, should be idempotent where possible.

Status codes:

2xx: success.
3xx: redirect/cache/navigation.
400: client contract wrong.
401: unauthenticated.
403: authenticated but not allowed.
404: not found or intentionally hidden.
409: conflict with current state.
429: rate limited.
5xx: server or dependency failure.

Headers that matter:
Authorization
Cookie
Set-Cookie
Cache-Control
ETag
If-None-Match
Content-Type
Accept
Origin
Access-Control-*
X-Request-Id / traceparent
CORS And Same-Origin Policy
Same-origin policy says browser JavaScript can read responses only from allowed origins. CORS is the server’s controlled exception.
Origin is:
scheme + host + port
So these are different origins:
http://localhost:3000
http://localhost:8080
https://localhost:3000
CORS is not authentication. It is a browser read-permission mechanism. A server still needs authentication and authorization.
Common CORS confusion:

Browser shows CORS error, but the real server returned 500 without CORS headers.
Cookies are not sent unless the fetch uses credentials and the server allows credentials.
Access-Control-Allow-Origin: * cannot be used with credentials.
Preflight OPTIONS must be handled by the server/proxy.

Debugging Checklist
curl -v app.example.com/api/me
curl -v -H "Origin: app.example.com" api.example.com/me
openssl s_client -connect app.example.com:443 -servername app.example.com
Read from the bottom up:

Did DNS resolve?
Did TCP/QUIC connect?
Did TLS verify?
Did HTTP route?
Did auth pass?
Did the app and dependencies succeed?

Sources

MDN HTTP
MDN TLS configuration
MDN CORS

03-AUTH-SESSIONS-COOKIES-OAUTH.title: 03-AUTH-SESSIONS-COOKIES-OAUTH
03-AUTH-SESSIONS-COOKIES-OAUTH.content:
Authentication, Sessions, Cookies, And OAuth
Auth feels puzzling because several questions are often collapsed into one word. Keep them separate and the system becomes tractable.
Cross-links: request trace, TLS, security, backend APIs.
The Four Questions






























QuestionNameExampleWho are you?Authenticationpassword, passkey, SSO loginWhat can you do?Authorizationrole, permission, tenant membershipHow do you prove it on this request?Credential presentationsession cookie, bearer token, mTLS certWho issued that proof?Trust authorityyour app, IdP, auth service, cloud IAM
Never design “auth” as one blob. Draw these four boxes.
Session Cookies
Classic browser app:
sequenceDiagram
  participant B as Browser
  participant APP as App
  participant S as Session Store
  B->>APP: POST /login username/password
  APP->>S: create session id -> user id
  APP-->>B: Set-Cookie: __Host-session=abc; Secure; HttpOnly; SameSite=Lax; Path=/
  B->>APP: GET /dashboard Cookie: __Host-session=abc
  APP->>S: lookup abc
  S-->>APP: user id + session metadata
  APP-->>B: dashboard

Server-side session properties:

Browser stores opaque session ID.
Server stores session state.
Logout can invalidate immediately.
Easy to rotate and revoke.
Requires shared session store if app servers are stateless.

Secure cookie baseline:
Set-Cookie: __Host-session=<opaque-random-id>; Path=/; Secure; HttpOnly; SameSite=Lax; Max-Age=3600
Cookie attributes:

Secure: send only over HTTPS.
HttpOnly: JavaScript cannot read it.
SameSite=Lax/Strict/None: controls cross-site sending.
Path: narrows where cookie is sent.
no Domain with __Host-: prevents subdomain cookie injection.

JWTs And Bearer Tokens
JWTs are signed claims, not magic sessions.
header.payload.signature
Good uses:

API access token with short lifetime.
stateless verification by resource server.
machine-to-machine calls.
signed ID token from OIDC provider.

Gotchas:

A stolen bearer token is usable by whoever holds it until expiration.
Revocation is hard unless you add a server-side denylist/introspection.
Do not store long-lived tokens in browser localStorage if XSS is plausible.
Validate issuer, audience, expiration, signature, and algorithm.
Do not treat an OAuth access token as proof that the user is logged into your app unless the flow is designed for it.

OAuth And OIDC
OAuth answers: “Can this client access this resource on behalf of this user or itself?”
OIDC adds: “Who is the user?”
Authorization Code + PKCE shape:
sequenceDiagram
  participant U as User
  participant B as Browser
  participant APP as Client App
  participant IDP as Authorization Server / IdP
  participant API as Resource API

  U->>APP: click login
  APP-->>B: redirect to IdP with code_challenge, state, nonce
  B->>IDP: authenticate user
  IDP-->>B: redirect back with authorization code
  B->>APP: code + state
  APP->>IDP: exchange code + code_verifier
  IDP-->>APP: id_token + access_token + optional refresh_token
  APP->>API: Authorization: Bearer access_token

Terms:

Authorization server / IdP: login and token issuer.
Client: application requesting tokens.
Resource server: API accepting access tokens.
ID token: says who authenticated.
Access token: authorizes API access.
Refresh token: obtains new access tokens, must be strongly protected.
state: CSRF/mix-up protection for redirect.
nonce: binds ID token to auth request.
PKCE: prevents authorization code interception/injection.

Browser App Choices
Server-rendered or BFF Pattern
Browser gets an HttpOnly session cookie for your backend. Backend stores/uses tokens server-side.
Pros:

tokens hidden from JavaScript.
CSRF defenses are well-understood.
easy logout/revocation.

Cons:

backend must exist for frontend.
cross-domain deployment requires cookie/CORS care.

SPA With Token In Browser
Browser JavaScript holds access token, often using Authorization Code + PKCE.
Pros:

direct API calls.
decoupled frontend/API.

Cons:

XSS can steal or use tokens.
refresh token protection is tricky.
logout and revocation are harder.

Default for high-value SaaS: use a BFF/server session unless you have a strong reason not to.
CSRF vs XSS
CSRF:

Attacker causes victim browser to send a request with victim cookies.
Browser auto-sends cookies.
Server must verify intent: CSRF token, Origin/Referer checks, Fetch Metadata, SameSite.

XSS:

Attacker runs JavaScript in your origin.
Can call APIs as user and read non-HttpOnly data.
HttpOnly protects cookie value from being read, but not from being used by same-origin malicious JS.

CSRF defense example:
session cookie: HttpOnly, SameSite=Lax or Strict
csrf token: separate server-generated token embedded in form or returned by endpoint
unsafe methods: require token + Origin/Fetch Metadata checks
Authorization
Authentication gives you a principal. Authorization decides if the principal may do the action.
Authorization input:
subject: user/service
tenant: org/account/project
resource: invoice/experiment/model
action: read/update/delete/invite
context: owner? IP? time? plan? risk?
Pattern:
def can_update_invoice(user, invoice):
    return (
        user.tenant_id == invoice.tenant_id
        and user.has_permission("invoice:update")
        and not invoice.locked
    )
Gotcha: multi-tenant authorization bugs often look like missing tenant_id filters, not fancy crypto problems.
Auth Debugging
When login/session breaks:

Did login endpoint return Set-Cookie?
Did browser store the cookie? Check domain/path/SameSite/Secure.
Is the request same-origin or cross-origin?
Does fetch use credentials: "include" if cross-origin cookie is intended?
Does server allow credentials in CORS?
Is TLS required for Secure or SameSite=None?
Does backend lookup session/token and map it to user?
Is failure 401 or 403?

Sources

OWASP Session Management
OWASP CSRF Prevention
MDN secure cookies
IETF RFC 9700 OAuth 2.0 Security BCP
OpenID Connect Core

04-BACKEND-APIS-AND-SERVICE-DESIGN.title: 04-BACKEND-APIS-AND-SERVICE-DESIGN
04-BACKEND-APIS-AND-SERVICE-DESIGN.content:
Backend Shape, APIs, And Service Design
A SaaS backend is a set of contracts around state. The language/framework matters less than whether the contracts are explicit: what each endpoint does, what state it reads/writes, how it authenticates, how it fails, and whether callers can retry safely.
Cross-links: request trace, auth, state, operations.
Monolith First, Boundaries Early
For many SaaS products, a modular monolith is the best starting point:
one deployable application
clear internal modules
one database boundary
well-defined API contracts
strong observability
You want service boundaries in the code before service boundaries on the network. A network hop adds latency, partial failure, versioning, tracing, auth, deployment coordination, and operational load.
Split into separate services when:

teams need independent deploy cadence.
scaling profile is genuinely different.
security boundary is different.
data ownership is clear.
failure isolation is worth the operational cost.

Do not split because “microservices are modern.”
API Styles



































StyleGood forWatch outREST/HTTP JSONbrowser apps, public APIs, CRUD-ish resourcesinconsistent semantics, over/under-fetchgRPCservice-to-service, typed contracts, streamingbrowser support/proxying, schema disciplineGraphQLclient-driven product surfacesauth complexity, N+1 queries, cache difficultyWebSocketsserver push, collaboration, realtimeconnection state, backpressure, deploy drainingEvent APIsasync workflowsidempotency, ordering, observability
Endpoint Contract Checklist
For every endpoint:
endpoint: POST /api/projects/{project_id}/runs
authn: required session or bearer token
authz: project:run:create in tenant
idempotency: Idempotency-Key required
request_body_schema: CreateRunRequest v3
response_schema: Run v3
side_effects:
  - inserts run row
  - enqueues worker job
timeouts:
  server: 2s
  dependency_db: 500ms
  dependency_queue: 300ms
retry_safe: yes, if same Idempotency-Key
audit_log: required
metrics:
  - request_count
  - request_latency
  - validation_error_count
If this feels heavy, remember: the production incident will reconstruct this contract anyway, but in panic.
Idempotency
Idempotency means repeating the same operation produces the same intended result.
Unsafe pattern:
client POST /charge
server charges card
network drops before response
client retries
server charges again
Safer pattern:
POST /charges
Idempotency-Key: 01HV...
Server stores:
tenant_id + idempotency_key -> request_hash + response/result
On retry:

same key + same request: return same result.
same key + different request: reject.

Timeouts, Retries, And Backpressure
Every network call needs:
timeout < caller timeout
bounded retries
jittered exponential backoff
circuit breaker or load shedding for dependency failure
Bad retry:
3 layers each retry 3 times -> 27 backend attempts
Better:

retry at one layer.
retry only safe/idempotent operations.
use request deadlines.
propagate cancellation.
return 429 or 503 when overloaded instead of timing out everything.

Versioning
Version the contract, not only the URL.
Useful tools:

explicit API schema.
additive changes first.
deprecation windows.
compatibility tests.
consumer-driven contract tests for important clients.

Breaking changes:

removing a field.
changing field meaning.
changing default sort/filter.
changing error shape.
changing auth/permission semantics.

Multi-Tenancy
SaaS usually means many customer organizations in one system.
Isolation models:






























ModelProsConsshared DB/shared schemasimple, cheaptenant filter bugs are dangerousshared DB/separate schemamoderate isolationmigrations harderseparate DB per tenantstrong isolationops and costseparate deployment per tenantstrongestexpensive, complex
Every query path should make tenant explicit:
SELECT * FROM runs
WHERE tenant_id = $1 AND run_id = $2;
Gotcha: background jobs and admin endpoints often bypass tenant filters.
Sources

MDN HTTP
Google SRE Service Best Practices
AWS Well-Architected pillars

05-STATE-DATA-CACHES-AND-QUEUES.title: 05-STATE-DATA-CACHES-AND-QUEUES
05-STATE-DATA-CACHES-AND-QUEUES.content:
State, Data, Caches, And Queues
State is what makes distributed systems hard. Compute can be scaled horizontally; state has memory, ownership, ordering, consistency, durability, and privacy. The SaaS mindset is to name each kind of state and choose how wrong it is allowed to be.
Cross-links: backend APIs, deployment, security, operations.
State Inventory
durable state:       database rows, object storage, ledger entries
session state:       login sessions, CSRF tokens, refresh tokens
cache state:         derived data, pages, API responses
queue state:         work to be done
client state:        browser memory/local storage/forms
config state:        feature flags, env vars, secrets
observability state: logs, traces, metrics
Ask “where is the state?” for every architecture. It reveals the scaling and failure story.
Database Role
For most SaaS apps, the primary relational database is the source of truth:

users.
tenants.
permissions.
billing.
product objects.
audit trails.

You do not need deep database internals to operate well, but you need:

indexes exist for query patterns.
transactions protect invariants.
migrations are backward-compatible.
backups and restore are tested.
slow queries are visible.
connection pools are bounded.

Gotcha: a database can be healthy while the app is down because the connection pool is exhausted.
Transactions And Invariants
An invariant is a statement that must remain true:
user cannot belong to another tenant's private project
invoice total equals sum of line items
job is processed at most once for billing side effects
Use transactions where invariants cross multiple writes.
BEGIN;
UPDATE accounts SET balance = balance - 100 WHERE id = 'A';
UPDATE accounts SET balance = balance + 100 WHERE id = 'B';
INSERT INTO ledger_entries (...);
COMMIT;
If a workflow crosses external services, a DB transaction is not enough. Use outbox patterns, sagas, or compensating actions.
Caches
Caches are lies with expiration dates.
Use caches for:

expensive reads.
remote API results.
rendered pages/assets.
session lookup acceleration.

Cache invalidation choices:

TTL: stale for bounded time.
explicit invalidation: precise but complex.
versioned keys: old keys naturally age out.
write-through/write-behind: stronger coupling.

Failure modes:

stale permissions after role change.
cache stampede when a hot key expires.
negative caching hides newly created objects.
caching personalized content at CDN edge leaks data.

Stampede mitigation:
single-flight: only one process recomputes key
jittered TTL: keys do not expire at the same instant
stale-while-revalidate: serve old value while refreshing
Queues And Background Work
Use queues when:

work is slow.
work can happen after response.
external dependency is unreliable.
throughput smoothing matters.

Examples:

sending email.
image/video processing.
report generation.
billing reconciliation.
webhook delivery.

Queue contract:
message_type: SendInvoiceEmail
idempotency_key: invoice_id + template_version
visibility_timeout: 5m
max_attempts: 8
dead_letter_queue: invoice-email-dlq
dedupe_window: 24h
Assume at-least-once delivery unless proven otherwise. That means the handler must tolerate duplicates.
def handle_invoice_email(msg):
    if already_sent(msg.invoice_id, msg.template_version):
        return
    send_email(...)
    mark_sent(...)
The Outbox Pattern
Problem:
write DB row
publish event
If the DB write succeeds and publish fails, state and events diverge.
Outbox:
sequenceDiagram
  participant APP as App
  participant DB as Database
  participant RELAY as Outbox Relay
  participant Q as Queue
  APP->>DB: transaction: write order + outbox event
  RELAY->>DB: poll unsent outbox events
  RELAY->>Q: publish event
  RELAY->>DB: mark sent

This gives a recoverable path because the event is durably stored with the business write.
Object Storage
Use object storage for:

uploads.
images/documents.
backups.
logs/exports.
large immutable artifacts.

Do not store large blobs in your relational database unless you have a specific reason.
Security rule:

app authorizes object access.
storage URL is short-lived or mediated.
object key should not reveal sensitive metadata.

Sources

AWS Well-Architected pillars
Google SRE Service Best Practices

06-CLOUD-CONTAINERS-AND-DEPLOYMENT.title: 06-CLOUD-CONTAINERS-AND-DEPLOYMENT
06-CLOUD-CONTAINERS-AND-DEPLOYMENT.content:
Cloud, Containers, And Deployment
Cloud infrastructure is rented primitives plus automation. The mindset shift is from “a server I log into” to “a declared system that can be replaced.” Compute is cattle; state and identity are the things you protect.
Cross-links: request trace, networking, state, operations.
Cloud Primitives

















































PrimitiveMental modelVMrented machinecontainerpackaged process filesystem + metadataimage registryartifact store for container imagesload balancertraffic distributor and health gateVPC/networkprivate address space and routing rulesIAMwho/what can call cloud APIsobject storedurable blob namespacemanaged DBdatabase with provider-run operationsqueuedurable async buffersecret managercontrolled secret distribution
Containers
A container is not a tiny VM. It is a process isolated by OS features and started from an image.
Good container contract:
FROM gcr.io/distroless/base-debian12
COPY app /app
USER 65532:65532
ENTRYPOINT ["/app"]
Runtime assumptions:

config comes from environment or mounted files.
logs go to stdout/stderr.
process handles SIGTERM.
app exposes health endpoints.
no local disk state is required for correctness.

Kubernetes Basics
flowchart TD
  DEP["Deployment: desired replicas"] --> RS["ReplicaSet"]
  RS --> P1["Pod"]
  RS --> P2["Pod"]
  RS --> P3["Pod"]
  SVC["Service: stable virtual endpoint"] --> P1
  SVC --> P2
  SVC --> P3
  ING["Ingress/Gateway: external HTTP routing"] --> SVC

Terms:

Pod: one or more containers scheduled together.
Deployment: manages rollout and replica count.
Service: stable network endpoint for changing Pods.
Ingress/Gateway: HTTP(S) routing from outside cluster.
ConfigMap: non-secret config.
Secret: secret config, still needs careful handling.

Minimal deployment shape:
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: api
spec:
  replicas: 3
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: api
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: api
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: api
        image: registry.example.com/api:2026-05-18
        ports:
        - containerPort: 8080
        readinessProbe:
          httpGet:
            path: /readyz
            port: 8080
        livenessProbe:
          httpGet:
            path: /healthz
            port: 8080
        resources:
          requests:
            cpu: "500m"
            memory: "512Mi"
          limits:
            memory: "1Gi"
Readiness means “send me traffic.” Liveness means “restart me if stuck.” Do not make liveness depend on a database outage or Kubernetes may restart every replica during an incident.
Deployment Strategies






























StrategyUse whenRiskrolling updatenormal backend releasesmixed versions must be compatibleblue/greenfast rollback, heavy changesdouble capacity temporarilycanaryrisk-controlled rolloutneeds metrics and routing controlfeature flagproduct behavior rolloutstale flags become complexity
Backward-compatible release pattern:
1. deploy code that understands old and new schema
2. migrate data/schema safely
3. switch behavior
4. remove old code later
Infrastructure As Code
Declare infrastructure:
resource "aws_s3_bucket" "uploads" {
  bucket = "example-prod-uploads"
}
Why it matters:

changes are reviewed.
environments can be recreated.
drift is detectable.
disaster recovery is realistic.

Gotcha: IaC can destroy things. Protect production state with deletion protection, backups, and review gates.
Environments
Common set:

local.
dev.
staging.
production.

Principle: staging should match production shape enough to catch operational bugs, but production data must not casually flow into staging.
Sources

Kubernetes Deployments
Kubernetes Services
Kubernetes Ingress
AWS Well-Architected pillars

07-SECURITY-THREAT-MODEL.title: 07-SECURITY-THREAT-MODEL
07-SECURITY-THREAT-MODEL.content:
Security Threat Model For Builders
Security is not a separate department-shaped spell. It is disciplined suspicion around trust boundaries, inputs, identity, secrets, and side effects. A practical SaaS engineer should be able to draw a threat model for a feature in five minutes.
Cross-links: auth, TLS, state, deployment.
Trust Boundaries
flowchart LR
  B["Browser: hostile environment"] --> EDGE["Edge / WAF"]
  EDGE --> APP["App: trusted code, untrusted input"]
  APP --> DB["Database: trusted storage"]
  APP --> PAY["Payment vendor"]
  APP --> EMAIL["Email vendor"]
  ADMIN["Admin console"] --> APP

Anything outside your server is untrusted:

browser JavaScript.
mobile app binaries.
request headers.
uploaded files.
webhooks unless verified.
internal services unless authenticated and authorized.

STRIDE In Plain English

































ThreatQuestionSpoofingCan someone pretend to be another user/service?TamperingCan data be modified without detection?RepudiationCan someone deny an action because no audit trail exists?Information disclosureCan data leak to the wrong party?Denial of serviceCan the system be made unavailable cheaply?Elevation of privilegeCan someone gain permissions they should not have?
Use this per feature, not only per system.
The Web App Top Risks You Actually Meet
XSS
Attacker runs JavaScript in your origin.
Defenses:

context-aware output encoding.
avoid unsafe HTML insertion.
Content Security Policy.
sanitize user-provided rich text.
HttpOnly cookies reduce token theft but do not stop same-origin malicious actions.

CSRF
Attacker makes victim browser send authenticated cookie-backed request.
Defenses:

no state changes on GET.
SameSite cookies.
CSRF token for unsafe methods.
Origin/Referer and Fetch Metadata checks.

SQL/Command Injection
Attacker turns input into code.
Defenses:

parameterized queries.
no shell string concatenation.
allowlists for commands and file paths.

SSRF
Server fetches attacker-chosen URL and reaches internal resources.
Defenses:

allowlist destinations.
block link-local/private/internal IP ranges.
resolve DNS safely and re-check after redirects.
do not attach cloud metadata credentials to arbitrary fetchers.

Broken Access Control
The most mundane and common SaaS disaster:
GET /api/projects/123
returns project from another tenant
Defenses:

make tenant/resource authorization explicit.
centralize policy.
test cross-tenant negative cases.
audit admin paths and background jobs.

Secrets
Secret rules:

never commit secrets.
store in a secret manager or encrypted CI/CD variable.
rotate on exposure.
scope narrowly.
prefer short-lived credentials.
log secret fingerprints, not values.

Bad:
DATABASE_URL=postgres://admin:password@...
Better:
app identity -> cloud IAM -> secret manager -> short-lived DB credentials
TLS And Internal Trust
TLS protects traffic in transit, but it does not decide authorization. A service with a valid cert can still call the wrong endpoint unless app-layer authorization checks the caller.
mTLS is useful for service identity, but you still need:

which service is allowed to call which API.
which user/tenant context is being propagated.
audit logs.

Security Review Prompt
For any feature:
What user data does it touch?
Who can call it?
How is caller identity proven?
What authorization check runs?
What input is parsed?
What external calls happen?
What state changes?
Can the operation be replayed?
What is logged?
What happens if a dependency is malicious or down?
Sources

OWASP XSS Prevention
OWASP CSRF Prevention
OWASP SSRF Prevention
NIST Digital Identity Guidelines

08-OBSERVABILITY-RELIABILITY-OPERATIONS.title: 08-OBSERVABILITY-RELIABILITY-OPERATIONS
08-OBSERVABILITY-RELIABILITY-OPERATIONS.content:
Observability, Reliability, And Operations
Modern SaaS is not done when the code works locally. It is done when you can tell whether users are succeeding, find why they are not, and change the system without gambling. Observability is the sensory system; reliability is the promise; operations is the practice.
Cross-links: request trace, deployment, backend, product scaling.
The Three Signals
OpenTelemetry organizes telemetry into:

traces: one request/workflow across components.
metrics: numeric time series.
logs: timestamped events.

flowchart LR
  APP["App"] --> TRACE["Traces: request path"]
  APP --> MET["Metrics: rates, latency, saturation"]
  APP --> LOG["Logs: facts and errors"]
  TRACE --> OTEL["OpenTelemetry Collector"]
  MET --> OTEL
  LOG --> OTEL
  OTEL --> BACKEND["Observability backend"]

Trace answers: where did this request spend time?
Metric answers: is the system healthy over time?
Log answers: what happened at this point?
Golden Signals
For a web service:
traffic: requests/sec
errors: error rate by class
latency: p50/p95/p99, from user perspective
saturation: CPU/memory/connections/queue depth/DB pool
Do not average latency only. Tail latency is what users complain about.
SLOs And Error Budgets
SLO: target reliability, e.g.
99.9% of successful authenticated dashboard requests under 500 ms over 30 days
Error budget:
allowed badness = 100% - SLO
Why it matters:

If you are burning budget, reduce risky launches and fix reliability.
If you have budget, ship improvements.
Product and engineering get a shared language.

Bad SLO:
CPU < 80%
Good SLO:
99.5% of checkout attempts complete successfully within 2 seconds
Health Checks
Use two checks:
/livez  - process is alive; restart if false
/readyz - instance can receive traffic
Readiness can depend on critical dependencies; liveness usually should not. If the database is down and liveness fails, every pod may restart and make the incident worse.
Incident Loop
detect -> triage -> mitigate -> resolve -> learn
During incident:

appoint incident lead.
state current user impact.
stop speculative changes.
prefer mitigation over root-cause perfection.
record timeline.

After incident:

blameless postmortem.
concrete prevention actions.
alert/runbook improvements.
test the failure mode if possible.

Minimal Instrumentation
from opentelemetry import trace
 
tracer = trace.get_tracer(__name__)
 
def handle_request(req):
    with tracer.start_as_current_span("create_run") as span:
        span.set_attribute("tenant_id", req.tenant_id)
        span.set_attribute("endpoint", "POST /runs")
        # validate, authorize, write DB, enqueue job
Metrics to add first:
http_requests_total{route,status}
http_request_duration_seconds{route}
db_query_duration_seconds{query_name}
queue_depth{queue}
worker_jobs_total{type,status}
auth_failures_total{reason}
Logs:

structured JSON.
include trace/request ID.
no secrets or tokens.
include tenant/user identifiers carefully and consistently.

Sources

OpenTelemetry docs
OpenTelemetry signals
Google SRE Service Best Practices
Google SRE Service Level Objectives

09-PRODUCT-ENGINEERING-AND-SCALING.title: 09-PRODUCT-ENGINEERING-AND-SCALING
09-PRODUCT-ENGINEERING-AND-SCALING.content:
Product Engineering And Scaling Judgment
Modern SaaS infrastructure exists to serve product learning, not to cosplay as a distributed systems paper. The expert move is choosing when to keep a system simple and when a reliability, security, or scale boundary has become real.
Cross-links: backend APIs, state, deployment, operations.
Product-Speed Architecture
Early SaaS wants:

one clear app.
one primary database.
one deployment pipeline.
one observability story.
feature flags.
backups.
auth done properly.

Scale when evidence forces it:

p95 latency exceeds SLO.
database saturation is measured.
deploys are too risky because blast radius is too large.
team boundaries create delivery bottlenecks.
security/compliance requires isolation.

The Scaling Ladder
1. make it correct
2. instrument it
3. add indexes and remove obvious waste
4. cache hot reads
5. move slow work to queues
6. add replicas and load balancing
7. separate read/write paths
8. split services only at strong boundaries
9. shard/partition state when unavoidable
Jumping to step 8 before step 2 is how teams build systems they cannot debug.
Latency Budgeting
For a page or API:
target p95: 500 ms
browser/network: 100 ms
edge/load balancer: 30 ms
app compute: 120 ms
database/cache: 150 ms
external APIs: 80 ms
margin: 20 ms
If one external API has p95 800 ms, it cannot be on the synchronous path for a 500 ms SLO. Queue it, cache it, precompute it, or change the product interaction.
Cost Awareness
Cloud cost is usually:
compute + database + storage + network egress + observability + vendor APIs + human operations
Cost controls:

right-size resources.
autoscale only with sane min/max.
cache or batch expensive external calls.
expire logs/traces appropriately.
avoid high-cardinality metrics.
use separate environments consciously.

Gotcha: observability can become a major bill if every request logs huge payloads or metrics include user IDs as labels.
Feature Flags
Flags let you decouple deploy from release.
Use for:

canaries.
gradual rollout.
kill switches.
plan/tenant targeting.

Flag debt:

stale flags make code unreadable.
flags need owners and removal dates.
flags that alter schema/data semantics are dangerous.

Flag metadata:
name: new-report-builder
owner: reports-team
created: 2026-05-18
remove_by: 2026-07-01
default: false
risk: medium
Build vs Buy
Buy when:

it is not your differentiator.
security/compliance risk is high.
operational burden is large.
migration cost is acceptable.

Build when:

the domain is core.
vendor constraints block product direction.
cost at scale is prohibitive.
integration risk is lower than dependency risk.

Examples:

auth provider: often buy early.
billing: usually buy early.
core product workflow: build.
generic metrics backend: buy/use managed unless observability is your product.

Sources

AWS Well-Architected pillars
Google SRE Service Best Practices

10-PRACTICE-PLAYBOOKS.title: 10-PRACTICE-PLAYBOOKS
10-PRACTICE-PLAYBOOKS.content:
Practice Playbooks And Meeting Drills
The goal is not to memorize product names. The goal is to build reflexes. These drills turn the guide into practice.
Cross-links: request trace, auth, operations, cheat sheet.
Drill 1: Trace Any Click
Pick a button in a SaaS app and answer:
What URL is called?
Which origin sends it?
Which cookies/tokens go with it?
Is there a CORS preflight?
Where does TLS terminate?
Which backend receives it?
What authn/authz check runs?
What state changes?
What happens if the request is retried?
What metric/log/trace proves success?
Do this with browser DevTools Network tab open.
Drill 2: Explain Login Without Handwaving
Whiteboard:
unauthenticated browser
-> login redirect or POST /login
-> identity proof
-> session/token issued
-> credential stored/presented
-> backend validates
-> authorization checks resource/action
Then answer:

Is the credential readable by JavaScript?
Can it be revoked immediately?
What happens on logout?
What blocks CSRF?
What limits XSS damage?
What happens if two tabs are open?
What happens if the token expires mid-request?

Drill 3: Classify A 500 ms Latency Regression
You see p95 go from 250 ms to 800 ms.
Ask:
Is it all traffic or one route?
One region or all regions?
App latency or network/edge latency?
DB time or queue time?
New deploy or traffic mix?
Long tail or shifted median?
Are retries amplifying the load?
Use traces to split:
edge -> app -> DB/cache -> external API -> response serialization
Drill 4: Design A Safe POST Endpoint
Endpoint: POST /api/invoices/{id}/send
Required answer:
authn: required
authz: invoice:send on tenant
idempotency: required
body validation: strict
side effects:
  - audit log
  - enqueue email job
  - mark invoice send_requested
retry behavior: safe with same idempotency key
observability:
  - request trace
  - audit event
  - queue metric
security:
  - CSRF if cookie-backed browser route
  - no GET side effects
Drill 5: Draw The State
For any feature, draw:
browser state
server memory
session store
database
cache
queue
object storage
third-party state
Then mark:

source of truth.
derived/cache.
confidential.
tenant-scoped.
recoverable from backup.
safe to delete.

Drill 6: Make A Release Plan
For a risky feature:
1. add instrumentation
2. deploy dark code
3. enable for internal users
4. canary 1%
5. monitor SLO + product metric
6. expand gradually
7. keep rollback path
8. remove stale flag
If rollback requires a database restore, the plan is not safe enough.
Drill 7: Incident First Five Minutes
Say out loud:
User impact:
Start time:
Affected paths:
Current hypothesis:
Mitigation options:
Owner:
Next update time:
Do not begin by debating root cause.
Mini Case Studies
Cookie Not Being Sent
Likely causes:

wrong domain.
wrong path.
Secure over local HTTP.
SameSite=None without Secure.
cross-origin fetch missing credentials: "include".
CORS does not allow credentials.
looking under the wrong origin in DevTools.

API Returns 401 In Production Only
Likely causes:

load balancer strips Authorization.
TLS terminates and app sees wrong scheme.
clock skew breaks token expiration.
wrong issuer/audience in environment.
session store not shared across replicas.

Background Job Runs Twice
Likely causes:

at-least-once queue delivery.
worker crashed after side effect before ack.
visibility timeout too short.
missing idempotency key.

Sources

This page operationalizes the source set in SOURCES.

99-MENTAL-MODELS-CHEATSHEET.title: 99-MENTAL-MODELS-CHEATSHEET
99-MENTAL-MODELS-CHEATSHEET.content:
Mental Models Cheat Sheet
Use this as the meeting-room recall sheet.
Request Path
browser -> DNS -> TCP/QUIC -> TLS -> HTTP -> edge/LB -> app -> auth -> state -> response
If debugging, walk it in order.
The Five Questions
Where is the state?
Who is trusted to say this is the user?
What happens if this dependency is slow/down/duplicates?
What proves user-visible health?
What is the reversible next change?
Auth Vocabulary
authentication: who are you?
authorization: what may you do?
session: server-side remembered login
cookie: browser storage automatically sent by matching requests
JWT: signed claims format
OAuth: delegated authorization
OIDC: OAuth + user identity
CSRF: browser tricked into sending cookie-backed action
XSS: attacker script runs in your origin
Secure Cookie Baseline
Set-Cookie: __Host-session=<opaque>; Path=/; Secure; HttpOnly; SameSite=Lax; Max-Age=3600
Use SameSite=Strict for more protection if UX allows. Use SameSite=None; Secure only when cross-site cookies are required.
HTTP Status Reflexes
400 bad request shape
401 not authenticated
403 authenticated but forbidden
404 not found / hidden
409 state conflict
429 rate limited
500 app failed
502 proxy could not talk to backend
503 unavailable / overloaded
504 upstream timeout
Retry Rules
retry only when operation is safe or idempotent
use timeouts
use jittered backoff
avoid retry multiplication across layers
carry idempotency keys for side-effecting POSTs
State Rules
database = source of truth
cache = derived and disposable
queue = at-least-once unless proven otherwise
object store = durable blobs
session store = login continuity
browser storage = user-controlled environment
Deployment Rules
readiness means send traffic
liveness means restart process
logs go stdout/stderr
config outside image
secrets outside code
roll forward with compatible schema
rollback must not require time travel
Reliability
SLI = measured user experience
SLO = target
error budget = allowed failure
trace = path of one request
metric = health over time
log = event details
Security Reflexes
validate input
encode output
parameterize queries
authorize every resource action
scope secrets and tokens
do not trust forwarded headers except from known proxies
no state changes on GET
audit admin actions
Scaling Ladder
correct -> instrument -> index/optimize -> cache -> queue -> replicate -> split reads/writes -> split services -> shard
Do not skip instrumentation.
Sources

SOURCES

index.title: index
index.content:
Modern SaaS Infrastructure Mindset
This guide is for a strong numerical/C++/MATLAB engineer who wants the operating model of modern SaaS software: requests, identity, TLS, sessions, APIs, cloud deployment, state, security, observability, and reliability. It avoids deep database internals and instead teaches the “great subset” that makes web systems survive production.
The organizing device is a single request:
your laptop -> browser/app -> DNS -> TLS -> CDN/load balancer -> app server
-> auth/session -> database/cache/queue -> response -> observability -> operations
If you can trace this path, explain the trust boundaries, and predict the failure modes, most SaaS architecture stops being intimidating.
Reading Path

Trace A Request End To End
Networking, HTTP, DNS, And TLS
Authentication, Sessions, Cookies, And OAuth
Backend Shape, APIs, And Service Design
State, Data, Caches, And Queues
Cloud, Containers, And Deployment
Security Threat Model For Builders
Observability, Reliability, And Operations
Product Engineering And Scaling Judgment
Practice Playbooks And Meeting Drills
Mental Models Cheat Sheet

Dependency Graph
flowchart TD
  R["01 Request Trace"] --> N["02 Networking / TLS / HTTP"]
  R --> A["03 Auth / Sessions / OAuth"]
  R --> B["04 Backend / APIs"]
  B --> S["05 State / Data / Queues"]
  B --> D["06 Cloud / Deployment"]
  N --> SEC["07 Security"]
  A --> SEC
  S --> SEC
  D --> O["08 Observability / Reliability"]
  B --> O
  O --> P["09 Product / Scaling Judgment"]
  P --> PLAY["10 Practice Playbooks"]
  PLAY --> C["99 Mental Models"]

The Five Questions To Carry Into Every Meeting

Where is the state?
Who is trusted to say this is the user?
What happens when this dependency is slow, unavailable, or returns twice?
What data proves the system is healthy from the user’s point of view?
What is the smallest reversible change that moves the product forward?

Source Grounding
The maintained bibliography is in SOURCES.
